content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 14, 1976


John R. Graf
Superintendent and
Secretary of the Board
Winters Joint Unified School District
47 Main Street
Winters, California 95694

Dear Mr. Graf:

Senator Dunlap has provided our agency with a copy of your
letter of August 19, 1976, transmitting a resolution adopted by
the board of your school district. Essentially, the resolution
asks the legislature to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974
to exempt elected school board members from the financial disclosure
provisions of Government Code Sections 87100, et seq.

It is understandable that unpaid school board members
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose certain
of their private financial interests. However, under existing
law, school districts are governmental agencies; members of
boards of education as well as certain administrative personnel
within the school districts "make or participate in the making
of governmental decisions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect private financial
interests. Under these circumstances, the Act requires that
school districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable to
members and officials who are capable of affecting such financial
interests. Only those financial interests which could be affected
by the filer's official actions must be disclosed, and it is
the school district which has the first and primary responsibility
to identify these potential conflicts of interest and provide
for their disclosure. Hopefully, individuals who object to
overly broad disclosure will take an active role in the design
of each code's disclosure requirements, so that intrusion of
personal privacy will be kept at a minimum consistent with the
objective of preventing or disclosing conflicts of interest.

The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to and
has provided technical assistance to state and local agencies
in drafting codes. In particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a
broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as
possible the particular types of financial interests which
could be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441


[mostly blank page with faint marks at top]

John R. Graf
September 14, 1976
Page Two


The Political Reform Act was, of course, passed as an
initiative. Section 81012(a) of the Act provides that the Act
may be amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes
are the following:

     Assets and income of public officials which
     may be materially affected by their official
     actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
     circumstances the officials should be dis-
     qualified from acting in order that conflicts
     of interest may be avoided.

          Gov. Code § 81002(g).

Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
school board members from the requirements of the Act could be
construed to be in furtherance of the purposes of the Act.

     Even assuming, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fully recognizing the right of school
board members to express their views, I believe the proposal
is without merit. School boards make decisions of great con-
sequence to their communities. Like other public officials,
school board members serve in a position of public trust, and
the people have the right to be assured that their decisions
will be made free from bias caused by own financial
interests. Disclosure of any financial interests which could
foreseeably be affected by their official actions is a reason-
able and effective means of preventing conflicts of interest.
In recent years the California Supreme Court and the courts of
many other states have ruled that such disclosure requirements
are valid and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of
the official is justified by the important public interest
that is served.

     I hope that this information is helpful. If you have
further questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
our Conflicts of Interest Division, at (916) 322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

COMMITTEES:
EDUCATION
GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATION
VICE CHAIRMAN
NATURAL RESOURCES AND
CONSERVATION
REVENUE AND TAXATION

JOHN F. DUNLAP
FOURTH SENATORIAL DISTRICT

REPRESENTING SACRAMENTO, YOLO, SOLANO,
NAPA, AND SONOMA COUNTIES

CHAIRMAN, SENATE MAJORITY CAUCUS

California State Senate
September 2, 1976

Mr. John Graf
Superintendent and
Secretary of the Board
Winters Joint Unified School District
147 Main Street
Winters, California 95694

Dear Mr. Graf:

     Thank you for sending me a copy of your school board's
resolution, expressing the view that local school board
members should be excluded from the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act.

     The Act, at Section 87300, requires agencies to adopt and
promulgate a conflict of interest code. "Agency" at Section
82003 includes "local government agency". "Local government
agency" includes "school district". Section 82041.

     I sympathize with your view that these provisions are
burdensome on persons serving, in many cases, without any
remuneration at all. I assume, however, the drafters of the
initiative (and perhaps the public in voting for the initiative)
perceived the real threat of conflicts of interest in awarding
contracts in the school district as well as any other legis-
lative or administrative body. In fact, it is arguable, that
since such a large percentage of a school board's business is
awarding contracts, such boards should be included.

     In any event, since the Political Reform Act was an initi-
ative measure, it can only be amended by a vote of the people,
except an amendment "to further its purposes" could be passed
by a two-thirds vote of each House of the Legislature. A
question arises as to whether such an amendment would be construed
to "further its purposes". For the views of the Fair Political
Practices Commission, I am forwarding this letter to Robert
Stern, General Counsel.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        John F. Dunlap
                                        Senator-Fourth District

AUG 2 3 RECD

WINTERS JOINT UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT
47 MAIN ST.        WINTERS, CA. 95694        795-4588

BOARD OF TRUSTEES

MRS. VERA M. HERRALA
PRESIDENT

GEORGE H. CRUM
CLERK

GEORGE J. CASTRO
HARRY L. FREDERICKS, JR.
GILBERT A. SEBASTIAN

JOHN R. GRAF, DISTRICT SUPERINTENDENT

WILLIAM H. OBER, FEDERAL PROJECTS DIRECTOR

PRINCIPALS

PAUL M. BROUGHTON
WINTERS HIGH SCHOOL

JOHN BALSHOR
WINTERS INTERMEDIATE
WINTERS ELEMENTARY

PAUL IRIBARNE
WAGGONER ELEMENTARY
WOLFSKILL KINDERGARTEN

August 19, 1976


Senator John Dunlap
Fourth Senatorial District
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Senator Dunlap:

     The Board of Trustees of the Winters Joint Unified School District, at its
August 5, 1976 board meeting passed a Resolution supporting the TAFB City School
District's position in opposition to the inclusion of unpaid school board members
within the requirements of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974."

     The Board feels that the Conflict of Interest Code is an infringement on the
rights and personal privacy of elected school board members and their immediate
families.  School Boards need citizens of the community with financial solvency
and personal stability to serve as board members, and enactment of the Code will
result in the resignation of untold numbers of board members throughout the State.

     We urge you to take the necessary action to bring about the changes needed to
exclude school board members from the requirements of the California "Political
Reform Act of 1974."

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        BOARD OF TRUSTEES
                                        WINTERS JOINT UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT

                                        [signature]

                                        John R. Graf
                                        (Superintendent and Secretary to the Board

jb


RESOLUTION

1          WHEREAS, public school boards are required by public law (Government

2    Code Sections 87100 et seq.) to prepare and adopt a district Conflict of

3    Interest Code, and

4          WHEREAS, board members of said school boards are required by law to

5    file a disclosure statement disclosing their interest and those of their

6    immediate families in investments, real property, and income, and

7          WHEREAS, these disclosure statements are by law made available for

8    public scrutiny, and

9          WHEREAS, other sections of the Government Code (Section 1090 et seq.)

10   and Title 2, California Administrative Code (Section 18700 et seq.) and the

11   Education Code (Section 1174) provide the needed legal control of board

12   member actions, and

13          WHEREAS, the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" is an infringe-

14   ment on the rights of private citizens and their immediate families and is

15   an invasion of personal privacy of board members and their immediate families,

16   and

17          WHEREAS, this act can require personal expenses for attorney and

18   accountant fees by board members holding non-paying elective board positions,

[Remainder of page is blank]

AND CHAIRMAN

AGING AND HEALTH PROFESSIONS

SPECIAL SUBCOMMITTEE

ADVISORY HEALTH COUNCIL
WILDLIFE CONSERVATION BOARD
WESTERN STATES FORESTRY
TASK [illegible]

ON

HEALTH CARE INVESTIGATIONS

August 17, 1976

Guy E. Poorman
District Superintendent
Rohnerville School District
2800 Thomas Street
Fortuna, California 95540

Dear Mr. Poorman:

Thank you for advising me that the Taft City School
District has adopted a resolution indicating the members'
belief that their being required to file a financial disclosure
statement is an infringement on their rights as private citizens.

Since this requirement stems from the "Political Reform
Act of 1974", I have taken the liberty of sharing your letter
with Daniel Lowenstein, Chairman of the Fair Political Practices
Commission. And, by copy of this letter, I am asking him to reply
directly to you regarding the continued need for such reporting.

If I can be of assistance with this or other matters, do
not hesitate to contact me again.

Sincerely,

BARRY KEENE

Dear Mr. Hoosmond:

Thank you for inviting me to serve on the City Council. I am honored to do so and hope that I can be of service to you and our community.

I look forward to meeting you and other members of the council to discuss the issues confronting our community. I trust that we will work together in a spirit of cooperation to serve the people of the City as effectively and efficiently as possible. I am committed to the principles of good government and to the wise use of public resources.

Please feel free to contact me if there is anything I can do to assist you.

Sincerely,

Respectfully,

[illegible]

Dr. Roland Burris

cc: [mailed membersdirection]

Rohnerville School District
2800 THOMAS STREET
FORTUNA, CALIFORNIA
E. A. UNDERHILL, DISTRICT SUPERINTENDENT OF SCHOOLS
725-5197

KENNETH A. STEELE
PRESIDENT
SYLVIA F. JUTILA
VICE-PRESIDENT
JO ANNE FRICK
TRUSTEE REPRESENTATIVE
DARREL PETERSEN
CLERK
ODELL SHELTON, JR.

August 10, 1976

ROHNER SCHOOL
5350 ROHNERVILLE ROAD
FORTUNA, CALIFORNIA
725-4683

TODDY THOMAS SCHOOL
2800 THOMAS STREET
FORTUNA, CALIFORNIA
725-5197

The Honorable Barry Keene
Assemblyman, State of California
Room 2188, State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Keene:

The Governing Board of Rohnerville School District
has acted in regular meeting to agree with a resolution
adopted by the Taft City School District on 7/7/76.
This resolution, which I imagine has been addressed to
various state senators and assemblymen opposes the conflict of interest code where it affects school board
members.

You are of course, familiar with the conflict of
interest code and the Rohnerville Trustees do feel that
this Political Reform Act of 1974 is an infringement of
their rights as private citizens and an invasion of their
personal privacy as well as that of their immediate family.
In my own view, I am in complete accord with the concerns
expressed by the trustees. In my experience it has often
been difficult to find community leaders willing to accept
the burdens of school board membership with the concommitent expenses, time and effort related to that
service. Further, as a school administrator in California
for the past 19 years, I believe that a school district
is a direct reflection of the leadership, strength, and
commitment offered by school trustees. This law, in my
view, tends to make prospective board members shy clear
of accepting the responsibilities of this form of
community service.


Honorable Barry Keeng                    -2-                    August 10, 1976

     On behalf of the board and for myself, therefore,
I urge that you support any action needed to bring about
changes which would exclude school trustees from the
requirements of the Political Reform Act of 1974.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        ROHNERVILLE SCHOOL DISTRICT

                                        [signature]
                                        Guy E. Poorman
                                        District Superintendent
